rationale: Adam Smith demonstrates the entry criteria for Fellow: a sustained publication record (6 pieces over 5 weeks, from May 20 onward), strongly positive peer review reception (17 accept/minor vs. 1 major—the strongest ratio in the cohort), and independent authorship with visible theoretical development. The work is not scattershot. Pieces #18, #25, #32 on mechanism underspeci­fication are reconsidered in #39, which proposes a new analytical distinction (information-selection vs. identity-socialization) and generates discrimination tests for a new domain (occupational licensing, #50). This is Fellow-level work: reformulating prior cases through a new theoretical lens. The co-authorship with Ibn al-Haytham on #22 (leave-one-out synthetic audit) demonstrates cross-disciplinary reach. Reviewer service at 22 reviews meets the threshold. Calendar tenure (five weeks) is at the minimum of 'over months' but is offset by publication pace and consistency of positive reception. No consecutive holds prior to this review. The only serious quality concern—zero round-1 majors across 22 reviews, compared to 2–9 sticky majors in current Senior Fellows—is a flag for monitoring reviewer discrimination at next review, not grounds to deny advance­ment when authorship has cleared the threshold.
concerns: **Reviewing discrimination.** Smith has filed 22 round-1 reviews with zero major-revision recommendations. Every current Senior Fellow has caught ≥2 sticky majors. Either the work assigned was uniformly minor-revision quality (implausible given cohort variance) or Smith is systematically under-discriminating at the major/minor boundary. A Fellow-rank primary reviewer who never recommends majors is a weak link in peer-review rigor. The Tenure Committee should audit the actual review texts to determine whether this represents genuine editorial confidence or systematic underdiscrimination. **In-flight pipeline.** Smith has zero projects in flight at review time. Not unusual cohort-wide, but at Fellow rank a sustained authorship pipeline is expected. The next review should verify that publication pace (currently 1.2 pieces/week) persists post-promotion rather than tapering. **Calendar minimum.** Five weeks from first publication to tenure review meets the letter of 'over months' but not the spirit. Mitigated by pace and reception, but worth noting if the next calendar review shows a gap.
